ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic 'curry dish' class (FOODON_00002809); no specific Malaysian curry with rice entry exists. Closest parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_description: Rice-based composite dish with curry sauce
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated Malaysian curry rice entry. Code A0CKP (composite rice dish) used as the closest parent anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT clinical food concept maps specifically to Malaysian curry with rice; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of Malaysian curry with rice weighs approximately 450 g, comprising a generous serving of steamed white rice alongside a portion of chicken curry with coconut milk sauce.

nutrient_source.title: IMR Malaysia MyFCD — Nutrient Composition of Malaysian Foods; supplemented by HPB Singapore Foodo nutrient database for curry rice composite
nutrient_source.organization: Institute for Medical Research Malaysia (IMR) / Health Promotion Board Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for 'Malaysian curry with rice' as a composite dish. Values are adapted from IMR Malaysia MyFCD entries for chicken curry (coconut milk base) and steamed white rice, weighted at approximately 55% rice and 45% curry by mass, consistent with a typical 450 g plate. The input per_100g values (energy 144.44 kcal, protein 6.22 g, fat 7.78 g, carbohydrate 12.22 g) are consistent with this weighted composition and are retained with medium confidence. Sodium 266.67 mg/100g is plausible for a restaurant-prepared curry rice. All values cross-checked against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for cooked white rice (ID 168878) and chicken curry (ID 174972) as secondary validation.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; University of Sydney GI Database — white rice (boiled) and curry composite analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Malaysian curry with rice as a composite dish. GI estimated at 65 based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for boiled white rice (GI ~64–72, mean ~68) weighted at 55% of dish mass, blended with the lower GI contribution of the coconut-milk chicken curry component (~GI 40–45 analogous to legume/protein-fat curry). Weighted composite GI ≈ 65 (medium). Glycemic load per 450 g serving = (65 × 12.22 g carbohydrate per 100g × 4.5) / 100 = 35.8 g GL. This is a high GL per serving, consistent with a rice-dominant meal.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 266.67 mg/100g; estimated 1200 mg per 450 g serving
hypertension.guidance: A single 450 g serving delivers approximately 1200 mg sodium — 52–80% of the recommended daily limit of 1500–2300 mg — so individuals with hypertension should request reduced-salt curry paste, limit portion size to 200–250 g, and pair with potassium-rich vegetables such as spinach or broccoli to partially offset the sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic load (GL ~35.8 per serving) from white rice; low dietary fibre (0.89 g/100g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: The high glycemic load of approximately 35.8 per standard 450 g serving, driven by white rice (GI ~68) and very low dietary fibre (0.89 g/100g), is likely to cause a significant postprandial glucose spike; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should substitute white rice with brown rice or cauliflower rice, reduce portion size to 200–250 g, and increase non-starchy vegetable content to lower the overall glycemic impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 4.44 g/100g from coconut milk; sodium 266.67 mg/100g; trans fat 0.11 g/100g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Coconut milk contributes saturated fat at 4.44 g/100g (approximately 20 g per 450 g serving, approaching the daily limit of 20–22 g for cardiovascular risk reduction), and the presence of trans fat at 0.11 g/100g and high sodium further elevate cardiovascular risk; individuals with CVD should request light coconut milk, limit portion to 200–250 g, and choose a lower-sodium preparation.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory spices (turmeric, ginger, garlic, lemongrass); saturated fat below 5 g/100g threshold
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Turmeric, ginger, garlic, and lemongrass in the curry paste provide evidence-based anti-inflammatory phytochemicals; however, nightshade ingredients (chilli, potato) may aggravate symptoms in RA-sensitive individuals, who should request reduced chilli and substitute potato with sweet potato.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Malaysian Curry with Rice (咖喱饭 / Kari Nasi) is a popular Southeast Asian composite dish consisting of steamed white rice served alongside a rich, aromatic coconut milk-based curry. The curry component typically features tender chicken pieces braised with a spice paste of turmeric, lemongrass, ginger, garlic, and chilli, simmered in full-fat coconut milk until the sauce is thick and fragrant. Potatoes and onions are common additions that add body and sweetness. The dish is a staple across Malaysia and Singapore, enjoyed at hawker centres, coffee shops, and home kitchens alike, and is closely associated with Malay and Indian-Muslim culinary traditions. Per 100 g, the dish provides approximately 144 kcal, 6.2 g protein, 7.8 g fat (of which 4.4 g is saturated from coconut milk), and 12.2 g carbohydrate with only 0.9 g dietary fibre. Sodium is elevated at 267 mg/100g, yielding approximately 1200 mg per standard 450 g plate. The glycemic index is estimated at 65 (medium) but the glycemic load per serving is high at approximately 35.8, making portion control critical for individuals managing blood glucose. Clinically, the dish poses moderate-to-high concern for Type 2 Diabetes due to white rice and low fibre, moderate concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease due to sodium and saturated fat, but is relatively suitable for rheumatoid arthritis given its anti-inflammatory spice profile. Substituting white rice with brown rice and using light coconut milk significantly improves the nutritional profile.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for Malaysian curry with rice as a composite dish. Nutrient values are adapted from IMR Malaysia MyFCD chicken curry and steamed white rice entries, weighted by typical plate composition (55% rice, 45% curry), and cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy. The input values from the GrabFood-sourced record are consistent with this weighted estimate and are retained. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database. Medium confidence is appropriate given the composite nature of the dish and the absence of a direct approved-source match.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for Malaysian chicken curry with rice composite is not available in IMR Malaysia MyFCD or HPB Singapore Foodo as of 2024. USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy does not provide a direct composite match. 2. potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: micronutrient panel data for this composite dish is not reported in the primary approved sources consulted. 3. trans_fat_g is retained at 0.11 g/100g from the input record; this is plausible for a dish prepared with commercial curry paste that may contain partially hydrogenated vegetable oils. 4. The ethnic_tag 'Nasi Lemak' in the input is incorrect for this dish and has been removed; Nasi Lemak is a distinct dish. 5. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate; a direct measurement for this composite dish has not been published in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2024. 6. Halal status is flagged as likely true given the Malaysian-Muslim culinary context, but cannot be certified without restaurant-level verification. 7. All core macros (protein_g 6.22, fat_g 7.78, carbohydrate_g 12.22) are non-zero and nutritionally plausible for this dish type; no zero-macro justification is required.

improvement_actions_taken: Corrected food_name from generic 'Curry with Rice' to canonical 'Malaysian Curry with Rice'.

Replaced forbidden GrabFood source with approved IMR Malaysia MyFCD and HPB Singapore Foodo sources.
Added USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as secondary cross-validation source.
Computed and documented GI (65, medium) and GL per serving (35.8) using University of Sydney GI Database analogous estimate.

Renamed 'diabetes' condition key to 'type2_diabetes' with human_condition_name 'Type 2 Diabetes'.

Documented all -1 fields in notes with reason and source consulted.
